summary: Joel shares two brief updates: he's setting up a new software company (temporarily called PaxDigita) in New York City and comments on the state of startup office space. He also highlights that four of the most influential UI designers—Norman, Laurel, Tognazzini, and Nielsen—are now all working together at the Nielsen|Norman Group, comparing it to a historic intellectual dream team.
key_points: Startup office spaces in NYC were cramped and poorly named ('cages' and 'bullpens'), making it hard to attract top talent.
The four greatest UI design thinkers—Norman, Laurel, Tognazzini, and Nielsen—have converged at one group, which Joel sees as a historic event.
Joel recommends four essential UI design books as required reading for every software engineer.
The physical workspace matters for recruiting top talent—terminology and environment send signals about company culture.
money_quotes: Can you imagine trying to hire the world's top computer scientists to work in 'cages'?
Imagine the Institute for Advanced Study in the forties when Einstein and Feynmann were there. Now imagine if Newton and Kepler were there, too, and you get some idea of how exciting this is.
These four books are the 'Great Books' of UI design and should be required reading for every software engineer and UI designer.
If you asked experienced UI designers to list the world's top names in UI design, you would get surprisingly similar results. Norman. Laurel. Tognazzini. Nielsen.
It seems to have escaped everyone's notice that all four are working together now.
key_insight: The physical environment you offer talent matters as much as the work itself, and the convergence of top minds in a field is a rare event worth paying attention to.
